TTI‑Solutions VBS — Employee Onboarding

User & Administrator Guide

Company: TTI‑Solutions (Thompson Telecom Infrastructure Solutions) Tagline: Infrastructure with Integrity Guide version: 1.0 · Date: 2026‑06‑10 Applies to: the Onboarding module in the TTIS Dashboard

ⓘ

Document mode — Auto (as‑built + as‑designed). Everything written in plain present tense describes the software as it works today. A small number of steps are marked with a PLANNED — not yet available in this release banner; those describe the intended experience and are clearly separated from working instructions. Appendix A lists the exact build status of every capability.

1. Introduction

The Onboarding module turns the TTIS Employee Onboarding Policy v1.0 into a tracked, verifiable program inside the TTIS Dashboard. For every new hire it opens a single onboarding record that carries:

  • a phase timeline — preboarding → day one → week 1 → 30 → 60 → 90 → complete;
  • a policy‑derived task checklist (Attachment A), tailored to the hire;
  • a versioned acknowledgment ledger (Attachment B) for handbook, conduct, IT acceptable‑use and safety sign‑offs;
  • a training roster (Attachment C) with completion records and passing scores;
  • two hard access gates — CUI provisioning and time‑charging — that hold a person out of sensitive systems until the right training is recorded;
  • screening status, asset issuance, milestone check‑ins, deadline reminders, and a chain‑anchored completion certificate.

The module does not store personal data. It records completion state, dates, identities and pointers to documents that live in the personnel file — never Social Security numbers, dates of birth, I‑9 images, bank details or screening results.

Who this guide is for. Two readers:

  • Administrators — HR, Hiring Managers, the CEO, IT/Systems and the Security Officer, who create records, mark progress, release gates and certify completion.
  • New hires, who sign in to a self‑service view to see their own outstanding items.
✓

Tip. The module begins after a hire is authorized. It documents and gates the onboarding journey; it does not make hiring decisions.

2. Before you begin

Access and sign‑in

  1. Open the TTIS Dashboard in your browser and sign in with your dashboard username and password.
  2. In the left navigation, look for Onboarding. If you have onboarding access, it is an active link. If your role does not include onboarding, the item appears dimmed and is not clickable.
  3. Select Onboarding to open the board.

🖼 Screenshot: Left navigation with the "Onboarding" item highlighted

Roles at a glance

Roles at a glance — data table 1
If you are… You can…
CEO Do everything: create, update, release gates, waive training, complete, cancel, and export.
Field Ops Manager (Hiring Manager) View, update, and approve records in your own division — work tasks, record check‑ins, and complete.
Finance View records and export the compliance reports.
Field Crew (New Hire) View your own record only, and submit your own acknowledgments and tasks.
Business Development No onboarding access.
Auditor (read‑only flag) View and export only — no changes.

Section 6 gives the full table.

The verification prompt (step‑up)

Some actions are deliberately high‑assurance. When you release a gate, waive a training requirement, complete a record, or cancel a record, the system asks for a fresh step‑up verification — a one‑time multi‑factor (MFA) assertion — before it will proceed.

  • If you have not enrolled in MFA, you are prompted to enroll first.
  • If your last verification is stale, you are asked to verify again.

You will see a message such as “A fresh step‑up (MFA) assertion is required for this high‑value action.” Complete the verification and retry the action. See section 8.

›

Note. Reading the board, opening a record, marking ordinary tasks, recording training, capturing acknowledgments, and tracking screening do not require step‑up. Only gate release, waivers, completion and cancellation do.

3. The onboarding lifecycle at a glance

Every record moves through a sequence of phases, computed automatically from the hire’s start date — you never set the phase by hand. Open statutory items (for example, an overdue I‑9) hold the record back at the phase where they are due.

  PREBOARDING        DAY ONE        WEEK 1         DAY 30        DAY 60        DAY 90        COMPLETE

  (before start)     (start date)   (+7 days)      (+30 days)    (+60 days)    (+90 days)

       │                 │              │              │             │             │            │

  packet sent       I‑9 §1 & §2    role goals     benefits      60‑day        90‑day      certified

  screening init    payroll        site safety    check‑in*     check‑in      check‑in    & anchored

  provisioning      accounts/MFA   credentialing

  day‑one plan      PPE (field)

* The 30‑day check‑in is recorded during the day‑30 phase; check‑ins must be recorded in order (30, then 60, then 90).

Alongside the phase, each record has an overall status:

  • preboarding → the record is open and the start date has not yet arrived;
  • active → the start date has arrived and onboarding is under way;
  • completed → every required item is done and the record is certified;
  • cancelled → the onboarding was stopped (for example, a no‑show or a rescinded offer).

🖼 Screenshot: Record detail header showing the status pill and "phase:" pill

4. Administrator guide

Each workflow below states who can perform it, when it happens in the lifecycle, and what you need first, then walks the steps and closes with what success looks like.

4.1 Create an onboarding record

Who: HR or the CEO (the create permission). When: as soon as a hire is authorized — ideally before the start date. Before you begin: the person must already exist as an authorized employee in HR (the record references that employee). Have the start date, division, position, and the per‑hire applicability flags ready.

A record is created by submitting the new hire’s details to the onboarding service. On creation, the system instantiates a tailored checklist — only the tasks, courses, gates and screening processes that apply to this hire, based on the flags you set.

The applicability flags — what each one turns on

4.1 Create an onboarding record — data table 2
Flag What it turns on
Field (is_field) Job‑site safety tasks (PPE issue, site briefing), the site‑specific safety acknowledgment, the Safety Orientation expectation before covered work, and the OSHA outreach module.
Time‑charging (is_time_charging) The DCAA‑Compliant Timekeeping course and the time‑charging access gate — the person cannot submit timecards until that gate is released.
CUI‑authorized (is_cui_authorized) The CUI Handling and CMMC / NIST SP 800‑171 Awareness courses and the CUI‑provisioning access gate.
E‑Verify required (everify_required) An E‑Verify case task and screening row. This flag is off by default and only takes effect once the program is configured to enable E‑Verify.
Screening level (none / standard / contract) standard (or contract) adds the background screening task and process; contract additionally adds the HSPD‑12 / PIV credentialing task and process.
Labor‑standards coverage (none / sca / dba / both) The SCA / DBA Labor‑Standards Orientation course and the wage‑determination communication task.
Division Determines which managers can see and work the record.

Steps

  1. Provide the hire’s employee identifier (for example, EMP‑1024), start date (in YYYY‑MM‑DD form), and optionally the login user, division and position title.
  2. Set the applicability flags above to match the role.
  3. Submit. The system confirms the employee exists, then opens the record in preboarding and builds the checklist.

🖼 Screenshot: Create‑record form with the applicability flags

What success looks like: a new record appears on the Onboarding board in preboarding, pre‑loaded with exactly the tasks, courses, gates and screening rows that apply to the flags you set. Each item already carries a due date computed from the start date.

›

Auto‑creation on hire. When an authorized hire is recorded in HR with an onboarding directive attached, the system opens the onboarding record automatically using the same flags — you do not have to create it by hand. A hire recorded without that directive creates no record, so nothing is ever created by surprise.

One open record per person. If the employee already has an open (preboarding or active) record, the system refuses to create a second one and tells you which record is already open.

4.2 Reading the board and a record

Who: anyone with onboarding view access (managers see their division; new hires see only their own record). When: any time.

The board (left nav → Onboarding) lists active records. The page title is Onboarding, and the subtitle reads “Policy‑tracked onboarding per TTIS Employee Onboarding Policy v1.0.” By default it shows active records only; select show completed to include finished ones.

Each row shows:

4.2 Reading the board and a record — data table 3
Column Meaning
Employee The new hire (and position, if set). Select it to open the record.
Division The owning division.
Start date The hire’s start date.
Phase The current phase chip (for example, day one, week 1).
Status preboarding / active / completed / cancelled.
Open tasks Count of tasks not yet finished.
Overdue Overdue task count. If any overdue task is statutory, the badge reads, for example, “2 overdue (1 statutory)” in the alert style.
Acks outstanding Required acknowledgments not yet signed.
Next due The date of the next item due.

🖼 Screenshot: Onboarding board with phase chips and an overdue/statutory badge

Opening a record. Select an employee to open the record detail page, titled “Onboarding — EMP‑1024.” The header shows the position, division, start date, status and phase, plus the record identifier and the program template version. When the record is complete, the header also shows the start of the completion hash.

The detail page reads top to bottom as the onboarding story:

  1. Task checklist (Attachment A) — tasks grouped by phase.
  2. Acknowledgments (Attachment B) — outstanding documents and the signed ledger.
  3. Training roster (Attachment C) — required courses and their status.
  4. Access gates (§9 / §11) — held or released, with preconditions.
  5. Screening (§6 / §8) — background, credentialing and related processes.
  6. Issued assets (§14) — equipment and its recovery status.
  7. Milestone check‑ins (§5.4) — the 30/60/90 record.
  8. Completion certificate (§17) — appears once the record is certified complete.

What success looks like: you can see, at a glance, what phase the hire is in, what is overdue, what is statutory, and what is next.

4.3 Working the task checklist

Who: HR, the hiring manager (in division), or the CEO. A new hire can submit their own tasks (see section 5). When: throughout the program. Before you begin: open the record and find the task in the Task checklist (Attachment A) section. Each task shows its owner, policy reference, due date, status, and any evidence pointer.

Marking progress. A task moves through pending → in progress → complete (and may be blocked when something it depends on is not ready). To record progress, update the task’s status:

  1. Set the task to in progress when work starts (optional).
  2. Set it to complete when it is done.

Evidence pointers. Some tasks require an evidence pointer before they can be completed — an opaque reference into the personnel file (for example, a document locator), never the document itself. If a task requires evidence and none is on file, completion is refused with “task ‘…’ requires a doc_ref evidence pointer.” Add the pointer, then complete the task.

Dependencies. A task may depend on a screening being cleared (for example, HSPD‑12 / PIV credentialing depends on the credentialing screening). If you try to complete it before the screening clears, you see “task ‘…’ requires screening ‘…’ to be cleared.” Clear the screening first (section 4.7).

Statutory tasks cannot be waived. The two I‑9 tasks — Form I‑9 Section 1 (by day one) and Form I‑9 Section 2 employer verification — are marked statutory. They can never be skipped or waived, and they drive priority reminders. An overdue statutory task holds the record’s phase back until it is completed. When you complete a statutory task, the system records a tamper‑evident audit anchor on it automatically.

⚠Planned — not yet available.

PLANNED — not yet available in this release. A manual “waive this task” action for ordinary (non‑statutory) tasks is planned. Today, a task becomes waived only automatically — when you change an applicability flag so the task no longer applies (see section 4.1 and the note below). There is no button to waive an individual task by hand yet.

What success looks like: the task’s status chip changes to complete, its completion time is stamped, and the board’s Open tasks / Overdue counts drop.

›

Changing a flag re‑tailors the checklist. If you update an applicability flag on the record, the system adds any newly applicable tasks and automatically marks now‑inapplicable open tasks as waived, with a reason recorded (“flag change…: no longer applicable”). Statutory tasks are never auto‑waived.

4.4 Capturing acknowledgments

Who: the new hire (signing their own record) or HR/the hiring manager recording on the hire’s behalf. When: typically day one through week one. Before you begin: open the record. The Acknowledgments (Attachment B) section lists any Outstanding documents as warning chips, or states “All required acknowledgments are signed.”

The required set comes from the program configuration and always includes the Employee Handbook, Standards of Conduct / Code of Business Ethics, IT Acceptable Use Policy, and Workplace Safety Policies. Field personnel additionally have a Site‑Specific Safety Acknowledgment.

Recording an acknowledgment

  1. Identify the outstanding document in the Acknowledgments section.
  2. Record the acknowledgment for that document. The new hire may sign their own; HR may record it on the hire’s behalf.
  3. The signature is captured at the document’s current version and is immediately anchored in the audit trail.

When HR records on someone’s behalf, the ledger shows On behalf = yes, so it is always visible who actually signed.

Re‑issued documents. Acknowledgments are tracked per document version. If a document is re‑issued (its version changes), the new version becomes outstanding again and must be acknowledged afresh — a previously signed older version does not satisfy it.

What success looks like: the document moves out of Outstanding and appears in the signed ledger with its version, who signed, whether it was on someone’s behalf, the time, and a chain hash. Signing the same document version twice is refused with “‘…’ v… already acknowledged on this record.”

🖼 Screenshot: Record detail — Acknowledgments section, outstanding chips and ledger

4.5 Managing training

Who: HR, the hiring manager (in division), or the CEO. When: courses carry due dates relative to the start date; most fall in the first weeks. Before you begin: open the record and find the Training roster (Attachment C) section, which lists each required course, its policy reference, the basis on which it applies (for example, all, time‑charging, cui_authorized, field), its due date, status, and score.

Recording a completion

  1. Find the course in the roster.
  2. Set its status to completed. If the course has a passing score configured, provide a score.
  3. Optionally attach an evidence pointer (a training/certificate locator).

If a passing score is configured and the score you provide is missing or below the minimum, completion is refused with “course ‘…’ requires score ≥ N.” Record a passing score to complete.

Mandatory courses by flag

4.5 Managing training — data table 4
Course Applies to
Business Ethics & Conduct; Mandatory Disclosure Rule; Anti‑Kickback Act Awareness All hires
Cybersecurity Awareness & Acceptable Use All hires
EEO & Anti‑Harassment Orientation All hires
Safety Orientation All hires (field: before covered work)
DCAA‑Compliant Timekeeping Time‑charging hires — gates timecards
CUI Handling; CMMC / NIST SP 800‑171 Awareness CUI‑authorized hires — gate CUI provisioning
OSHA Outreach Module Field hires
SCA / DBA Labor‑Standards Orientation Hires with labor‑standards coverage

Waiving a course. A course may be waived only through the governed waive action, which requires the approve permission and step‑up verification, and records a written reason. The hard‑gate courses can never be waived: DCAA‑Compliant Timekeeping for time‑charging hires, and CUI Handling / CMMC‑NIST Awareness for CUI‑authorized hires. Attempting to waive one is refused with “course ‘…’ gates §9/§11 access and cannot be waived.”

What success looks like: the course status chip reads completed (or waived), the completion is anchored, and any access gate that depends on it can now be released (section 4.6).

🖼 Screenshot: Record detail — Training roster with statuses and scores

4.6 Releasing the gates

Who: the CEO or an approver with onboarding approve (in division). When: after the gate’s precondition courses are completed. Before you begin: open the record’s Access gates (§9 / §11) section. Each gate shows its type, its precondition courses, its status (held or released), and who released it.

The module enforces two hard gates. Their real‑world consequence is the point:

  • Time‑charging gate — created for time‑charging hires. Until it is released, the person cannot submit a timecard. If they try, timecard submission is refused with the message that DCAA timekeeping training must be completed and the time‑charging access gate released before submitting timecards. Its precondition is DCAA‑Compliant Timekeeping completed.

  • CUI‑provisioning gate — created for CUI‑authorized hires. Its preconditions are CMMC / NIST SP 800‑171 Awareness and CUI Handling, both completed with passing scores. Releasing it records that the person is cleared for CUI‑authorized provisioning.

Releasing a gate

  1. Confirm the precondition courses are completed (with passing scores where required).
  2. Choose release on the gate.
  3. Complete the step‑up verification when prompted.

The system checks the preconditions at the moment of release, all in one step. If any precondition course is not completed with a passing score, the release is refused and the message lists exactly which courses are missing (“courses not completed with a passing score: […]”). The gate stays held.

When release succeeds, the gate flips to released, records who released it and when, and writes a tamper‑evident audit anchor.

🖼 Screenshot: Record detail — Access gates section, "Release" action and preconditions

⚠Planned — not yet available.

PLANNED — not yet available in this release. Releasing the time‑charging gate has immediate, enforced effect today: a held gate blocks timecard submission. The CUI‑provisioning gate is fully tracked and released the same way, but the list of specific sensitive systems it will hold a person out of is still being finalized, so its downstream blocking is not yet switched on. Release it as part of onboarding regardless — the completion check requires it.

What success looks like: the gate status reads released; for the time‑charging gate, the person can now submit timecards.

4.7 Tracking screening

Who: HR or the Security Officer function (in practice, HR or the CEO), in division. When: background screening usually starts in preboarding; credentialing in week one. Before you begin: open the record’s Screening (§6 / §8) section. It lists each screening type (for example, background, hspd12 piv, everify case), its status, a case reference pointer where relevant, and when it was initiated.

A screening process moves through not required → initiated → in progress → cleared (or denied). Update its status as the external process advances. Only a pointer (such as a case number) is stored — never the screening result content, which stays in the personnel file.

Why “cleared” matters. Some tasks declare a dependency on a screening being cleared (for example, HSPD‑12 / PIV credentialing depends on the credentialing screening). While that screening is anything other than cleared, the dependent task is blocked and cannot be completed. Moving a screening to cleared unblocks its dependent work; moving it to cleared or denied is anchored in the audit trail.

What success looks like: the screening status reflects reality, and any dependent tasks can proceed once it is cleared.

🖼 Screenshot: Record detail — Screening section with statuses and case references

4.8 Issuing and recovering assets

Who: HR, IT, or the hiring manager (in division). When: typically day one onward. Before you begin: open the record. Issued equipment appears in the Issued assets (§14) section with its tag, type, status, responsibility acknowledgment, and issue time.

Issuing equipment

  1. Record the asset tag and asset type for the equipment.
  2. Issue it to the new hire. The issuance is audit‑logged.
  3. Capture the new hire’s responsibility acknowledgment when they accept the equipment.

An asset moves through issued → returned (or lost), or to recovery due when the person separates. You can record the responsibility acknowledgment and the return/loss status from the asset.

Automatic recovery at separation. When HR terminates or offboards an employee, the system automatically flags that person’s still‑issued assets as “recovery due” and raises a recovery notification — so the policy’s “assets recovered at separation” obligation surfaces on its own, without anyone remembering to check.

What success looks like: issued equipment is on the record with a responsibility acknowledgment; at separation, open assets switch to recovery due automatically.

🖼 Screenshot: Record detail — Issued assets section

4.9 Milestone check‑ins (30 / 60 / 90)

Who: the hiring manager (in division) or the CEO (the approve permission). When: at the 30‑, 60‑, and 90‑day milestones. Before you begin: open the record’s Milestone check‑ins (§5.4) section. Until any are recorded it reads “No check‑ins recorded yet (30/60/90‑day).”

Recording a check‑in

  1. Choose the milestone — day 30, day 60, or day 90.
  2. Optionally attach a summary pointer (a reference to where the check‑in notes are kept — never the notes themselves).
  3. Record it. The check‑in is anchored in the audit trail automatically.

Sequence rule. Check‑ins must be recorded in order. You cannot record the 90‑day before the 30‑ and 60‑day exist; attempting it is refused with “day_90 requires [‘day_30’, ‘day_60’] to be recorded first.” Recording the same milestone twice is refused as already recorded.

What success looks like: the milestone appears in the check‑ins table with who conducted it, when, and a chain hash.

4.10 Reminders and the sweep

Who: the CEO or a service account (the trigger permission) runs the sweep; everyone benefits from the reminders. When: on a regular cadence (for example, daily). Before you begin: nothing — the sweep reads open items and sends notifications.

The module does not run its own background timer. Instead, an administrator (or an automated trigger) runs a reminder sweep that:

  1. finds open tasks and courses whose due date is within three days or past;
  2. sends a notification for each — higher‑priority for statutory items (the I‑9 deadlines), routine priority for everything else;
  3. marks each item so the same reminder is never sent twice for the same stage (approaching vs. overdue).

Notifications carry only the record identifier, the employee identifier, and the item code — no names and no personal data.

Running a sweep

  1. Trigger the sweep action.
  2. The system returns a small summary — how many records it scanned and how many reminders it sent.

Re‑running is safe. Running the sweep again the same day sends nothing twice: items already reminded at their current stage are skipped, and a repeated trigger simply replays the previous result.

⚠Planned — not yet available.

PLANNED — not yet available in this release. The sweep’s automatic schedule (for example, a daily cloud or server timer) is a deployment‑time setup step that depends on where the system is hosted. The sweep action itself is fully available now and can be run on demand or wired to any external scheduler.

What success looks like: owners receive timely reminders for approaching and overdue items, and statutory deadlines get the higher‑priority path.

4.11 Completing and certifying (and cancelling)

Who: the CEO or an approver (in division). When: once every required item is done. Before you begin: open the record and confirm nothing is outstanding.

Completing a record. When you choose complete, the system verifies — before it will certify — that all of the following are true:

  • every task is finished (none pending, in progress, or blocked);
  • every required acknowledgment is signed at its current version;
  • every required course is completed or waived;
  • every access gate is released;
  • all three milestone check‑ins (30/60/90) are recorded.

If anything is still open, completion is refused with “record has open required items” and a breakdown of the counts (open tasks, incomplete training, gates held, outstanding acknowledgments, missing check‑ins). Resolve those, then retry.

Steps

  1. Choose complete on the record.
  2. Complete the step‑up verification when prompted.
  3. The record moves to completed, its phase becomes complete, and a verifiable completion certificate is anchored.

What success looks like: the record’s status reads completed; the Completion certificate (§17) section appears, stating “Onboarding certified complete … — chain‑anchored and verifiable,” and shows the completion hash. The certificate is the record itself plus its chain hash — verifiable at any time.

🖼 Screenshot: Record detail — Completion certificate section with completion hash

Cancelling a record (the alternate ending). If a hire does not start (no‑show or rescinded offer), choose cancel, provide a reason (required), and complete the step‑up verification. The record moves to cancelled and the cancellation is anchored. Completed and cancelled records are terminal — no further changes are accepted.

4.12 Reports and exports

Who: the CEO or Finance (the export permission); auditors may export read‑only. When: any time — for compliance review, audit, or records. Before you begin: decide the scope — a single record, a division, or firm‑wide.

The module generates the policy’s Attachment A/B/C as spreadsheet (CSV) exports, plus the printable certified record:

4.12 Reports and exports — data table 5
Export Contents
Checklist (Attachment A) Tasks across records: codes, titles, phase, owner, policy, statutory flag, status, due and completion dates.
Acknowledgments (Attachment B) Signed acknowledgments: document, version, who signed, on‑behalf flag, time, and chain hash.
Training (Attachment C) Course completion log: course, policy, status, score, passing score, completion time, and chain hash.

Steps

  1. Choose the export — checklist, acks, or training.
  2. Optionally scope it to a single record or a division.
  3. The file downloads (named onboarding_checklist.csv, onboarding_acks.csv, or onboarding_training.csv).

Every export contains identifiers, codes, dates, statuses and hashes — no names and no personal data — by construction.

The printable certified record. The record‑detail page, with the completion certificate and hash displayed, serves as the printable certified record. Use your browser’s print function to produce a clean copy.

What success looks like: you have a CSV (or printed record) suitable for compliance review and audit, free of personal data.

4.13 Administering the program configuration

Who: a privileged administrator (the program configuration is maintained alongside the system, typically by the CEO/IT). When: when policy content changes — new course, re‑issued document, changed due window, new clause reference.

The shape of the program — which tasks, courses, acknowledgment documents and gates exist, their due‑day offsets, the reminder window, and the clause references — lives in a single program configuration. Adjusting it changes what new records look like, without touching the software.

What the configuration controls:

  • Acknowledgment documents — their titles and current version strings. Re‑issuing a document means bumping its version here; that reopens the acknowledgment for hires.
  • Training courses — codes, titles, the flag each applies to, due‑day offsets, and passing scores (several passing scores are still pending HR confirmation).
  • Tasks — the Attachment A checklist: code, title, phase, owner role, policy reference, whether it is statutory, due‑day offset, and any screening dependency.
  • Screening processes, access gates and their precondition courses.
  • Phase offsets (the day counts for week 1 / 30 / 60 / 90).
  • Reminders — the three‑day approach window and the priority tiers.
  • Clause references and retention keys (kept as data; several await counsel confirmation).
  • E‑Verify — disabled until a contract clause requires it.

Two rules to remember

  1. Records keep their original shape. Each record stamps the template version in force when it was created. Changing the configuration later does not reshape existing records — they keep the tasks and courses they were created with. The header of every record shows its template version.
  2. Clause citations are never lost. Clause references are seeded once; a confirmed citation entered later is never overwritten by a redeploy.

What success looks like: new records reflect the updated program; existing records are unchanged and clearly stamped with the version they were built from.

5. New‑hire guide (self‑service)

Welcome to TTI‑Solutions. This short walkthrough shows you how to see your own onboarding progress.

You can see only your own record. The onboarding view is private to you — you cannot see anyone else’s onboarding.

  1. Sign in to the TTIS Dashboard with the username and password provided to you.
  2. In the left navigation, select Onboarding. You are taken straight to your own record (there is a “my onboarding” shortcut that finds your record for you).
  3. Read your outstanding items. Your record shows: - Task checklist — what is expected of you and by when (your tasks are marked with you as the owner); - Acknowledgments — any documents still outstanding appear as chips at the top of that section; - Training roster — your required courses and whether each is completed.
  4. Check your phase and status in the header — for example, day one or week 1.

🖼 Screenshot: New‑hire self‑view — own record with outstanding acknowledgments

Signing your acknowledgments and marking your tasks. You are permitted to submit your own acknowledgments and complete your own tasks (the ones that list you as the owner). When you sign a document, your signature is recorded against that document’s current version and time‑stamped.

⚠Planned — not yet available.

PLANNED — not yet available in this release. Signing acknowledgments and marking your tasks directly on the self‑service page (in‑page buttons) is planned. In this release the self‑service page is for reviewing your outstanding items. Until in‑page actions ship, your HR coordinator can record your acknowledgments and task completions for you — and when they do, the ledger shows it was done on your behalf, so there is always a clear record.

What success looks like: you know exactly what you still owe — which documents to sign, which tasks to finish, and which training to complete — and you can watch those items clear as onboarding progresses.

6. Roles & permissions reference

“Division‑scoped” means a Field Ops Manager can act only on records in their own division. “Own‑record only” means a Field Crew member can see and act on only their own onboarding.

6. Roles & permissions reference — data table 6
Action CEO Field Ops Manager Finance Field Crew (New Hire) Business Dev Auditor flag
View the board / a record ✓ ✓ (own division) ✓ ✓ (own record only) — ✓ (view only)
Create a record ✓ — — — — —
Update a record / flags ✓ ✓ (own division) — — — —
Work tasks ✓ ✓ (own division) — Submit own tasks — —
Capture acknowledgments ✓ ✓ (on behalf) — Sign own — —
Record / waive training ✓ ✓ update (own division) — — — —
Release a gate ✓ ✓ approve (own division) — — — —
Track screening ✓ ✓ (own division) — — — —
Issue / recover assets ✓ ✓ (own division) — — — —
Record check‑ins ✓ ✓ (own division) — — — —
Complete / cancel ✓ ✓ approve (own division) — — — —
Run the reminder sweep ✓ — — — — —
Export reports ✓ — ✓ — — ✓ (view/export only)

Notes:

  • The CEO can do everything, including all the HR, IT and Security‑Officer duties — the system currently maps those policy roles to the CEO and the division managers rather than to separate HR/IT accounts.
  • Waivers, gate releases, completion and cancellation additionally require step‑up verification (section 2).
  • Auditors (a read‑only flag on an account) may view and export but are denied every change.

7. Statuses & badges glossary

Record status

7. Statuses & badges glossary — data table 7
Status Meaning
preboarding Open; the start date has not yet arrived.
active The start date has arrived; onboarding is under way.
completed Every required item is done and the record is certified.
cancelled Onboarding was stopped (no‑show, rescinded offer); terminal.

Phase (shown as the phase chip): preboarding → day one → week 1 → day 30 → day 60 → day 90 → complete. The phase is computed from the start date and is held back by any open statutory task.

Task status

7. Statuses & badges glossary — data table 8
Status Meaning
pending Not started.
in progress Started, not finished.
complete Finished.
blocked Waiting on a dependency (for example, a screening to clear).
waived No longer required — set automatically when a flag change makes the task inapplicable (never for statutory tasks).

A statutory badge marks the non‑waivable I‑9 tasks. On the board, an overdue badge may read, for example, “2 overdue (1 statutory)”.

Training status: assigned → in progress → completed, or waived.

Gate status: held (preconditions not yet cleared / not released) or released.

Screening status: not required, initiated, in progress, cleared, or denied.

Asset status: issued, recovery due (flagged at separation), returned, or lost.

Acks outstanding / Open tasks / Overdue / Next due — board counters summarizing the record’s open items.

8. Common messages & how to resolve them

8. Common messages & how to resolve them — data table 9
What you see What it means What to do
“A fresh step‑up (MFA) assertion is required for this high‑value action.” This action (gate release, waiver, completion, cancellation) needs fresh verification. Complete the multi‑factor verification, then retry.
“MFA enrollment required before this high‑value action.” You have not enrolled in multi‑factor verification. Enroll in MFA, then retry.
A version conflict (the record changed since you loaded it). Someone updated the record after you opened it; your view is out of date. Reload the record and retry your change.
You are asked for a record version you didn’t supply. A change was attempted without the record’s current version. Reopen the record so the current version is loaded, then retry.
“waiving a task requires the waive endpoint” (when trying to set a task to waived). Tasks are not waived by changing their status; statutory tasks can never be waived. Don’t waive statutory tasks. For non‑statutory tasks, change the applicable flag so the task no longer applies (it is then auto‑waived).
“task ‘…’ requires a doc_ref evidence pointer.” The task needs an evidence pointer before it can be completed. Add the evidence pointer (a personnel‑file locator), then complete.
“task ‘…’ requires screening ‘…’ to be cleared.” The task depends on a screening that is not yet cleared. Move the screening to cleared, then complete the task.
“course ‘…’ requires score ≥ N.” The course has a minimum passing score and the score is missing or too low. Record a passing score to complete the course.
“course ‘…’ gates §9/§11 access and cannot be waived.” This is a hard‑gate course (DCAA timekeeping, or CUI/CMMC‑NIST). It cannot be waived — the course must be completed.
“courses not completed with a passing score: […]” A gate release was attempted before its precondition courses were finished. Complete the listed courses (with passing scores), then release the gate.
“DCAA timekeeping training must be completed and the time‑charging access gate released before submitting timecards.” The person’s time‑charging gate is still held. Complete DCAA timekeeping and release the time‑charging gate, then submit the timecard.
“day_90 requires [‘day_30’, ‘day_60’] to be recorded first.” Check‑ins must be recorded in order. Record the 30‑ and 60‑day check‑ins first.
“‘…’ v… already acknowledged on this record.” That document version is already signed. No action needed — it is already done.
“record has open required items: …” Completion was attempted while items are still open. Clear the listed open tasks, training, gates, acknowledgments and check‑ins, then complete.
“employee ‘…’ already has an open onboarding record (…).” The person already has an open record. Use the existing record instead of creating a second one.
“Forbidden: not your onboarding record.” / “you may only submit your own onboarding tasks.” A new hire tried to view or act on someone else’s record or task. You may only work your own record and your own tasks.

9. FAQ

Does the module store Social Security numbers, I‑9 images, or screening results? No. It stores completion state, dates, identities and pointers to documents that live in the personnel file. Notifications and exports contain no names and no personal data.

Who sets the phase? No one — the phase is computed from the start date. An open statutory item (like an overdue I‑9) holds the phase back until it is resolved.

Why can’t this person submit a timecard? Because their time‑charging gate is still held — DCAA timekeeping training must be completed and the gate released first.

Can the I‑9 tasks be skipped? No. They are statutory and can never be waived; they also drive higher‑priority reminders.

A document was re‑issued. Do people need to re‑sign? Yes. Acknowledgments are tracked per version; a new version reopens the requirement.

Is it safe to run the reminder sweep twice? Yes. It never sends the same reminder twice for the same stage, and a repeated run simply replays the previous result.

What is the “completion certificate”? It is the record itself plus its tamper‑evident chain hash, shown on the record once it is certified complete and available as a printable page and CSV exports.

Can a new hire see other people’s onboarding? No — the self‑service view is strictly limited to their own record.

What happens to issued equipment when someone leaves? When HR terminates or offboards the employee, their still‑issued assets are automatically flagged recovery due and a notification is raised.

Appendix A — Coverage & reconciliation map

Administrator / internal reference. Build status of every capability in the Product Design Specification (R‑OB‑01), verified against the source code. IMPLEMENTED = works today; PARTIAL = present with a noted limit; PLANNED = specified, not yet built.

Appendix A — Coverage & reconciliation map — data table 10
Capability (PDS) Status Where it lives (citation)
FR‑1 Record lifecycle & phase timeline IMPLEMENTED dashboard/onboarding.py create_record, _compute_phase, _refresh_phase (lines 413–448, 531–585)
FR‑1 Auto‑create on authorized hire IMPLEMENTED tools/hr_db.py:187–202 (opt‑in onboarding directive on hire/onboard)
FR‑2 Conditional resolution + flag re‑resolution IMPLEMENTED _applies, _instantiate_children, update_record (311–336, 453–522, 712–847)
FR‑3 Task checklist FSM, evidence, dependency IMPLEMENTED update_task (852–933)
FR‑3 Statutory I‑9 non‑waivable + strict anchor IMPLEMENTED update_task statutory branch (872–924); config statutory: true on i9_section1, i9_verification
FR‑3 Manual per‑task waiver endpoint PLANNED No POST /onboarding/tasks/{id}/waive route exists; tasks auto‑waive only on flag re‑resolution (update_record 788–800). PDS §10 lists the endpoint.
FR‑4 Acknowledgment ledger (versioned, on‑behalf, strict) IMPLEMENTED record_ack (947–994); duplicate → OnboardingConflict
FR‑5 Training roster, passing score, waiver IMPLEMENTED update_training, waive_training (999–1153)
FR‑5 Hard‑gate courses unwaivable IMPLEMENTED _hard_gate_courses + waive_training (1016–1133)
FR‑6 CUI / time‑charging gates, transactional release IMPLEMENTED release_gate (1168–1234)
FR‑6 Time‑charging keystone enforcement (blocks timecards) IMPLEMENTED held_gate_types (1237–1257); abac.gate_keystone_denied + GATE_GUARDED={"timecard":"time_charging"} (abac.py:183, 255–265); enforced at main.py:1029–1040
FR‑6 CUI‑provisioning keystone (downstream resource blocking) PARTIAL / PLANNED Gate is created/released; no resource mapped to cui_provisioning in GATE_GUARDED yet (config pending OQ‑10, abac.py:181–183)
FR‑7 Screening status tracking + dependency block IMPLEMENTED update_screening (1275–1325); dependency in update_task (887–895)
FR‑8 Asset issuance + responsibility ack IMPLEMENTED issue_asset, update_asset (1433–1496)
FR‑8 Separation recovery hook IMPLEMENTED flag_assets_for_recovery (1499–1529); HR hook tools/hr_db.py:171–178
FR‑9 Milestone check‑ins, sequence rule IMPLEMENTED record_checkin (1538–1581)
FR‑10 Completion verification + certificate IMPLEMENTED _completion_gaps, complete_record (1586–1648)
FR‑10 Cancellation (no‑show path) IMPLEMENTED cancel_record (1651–1673)
FR‑11 Idempotent reminder sweep IMPLEMENTED run_onboarding_sweep (1330–1411); endpoint main.py:2511
FR‑11 External scheduler wiring PLANNED Endpoint exists; the cron/Cloud Scheduler trigger is a deployment step (OQ‑1/OQ‑3) — no in‑repo scheduler
FR‑12 CSV exports (checklist/acks/training) IMPLEMENTED export_csv (1697–1745); endpoint main.py:2661
FR‑12 Printable certified record IMPLEMENTED onboarding_detail.html completion section (197–207)
API contract (404/409/400/422, OCC, step‑up) IMPLEMENTED main.py:2199–2209 mapping; _enforce_step_up (498–513) → 403 step‑up (PDS said 401; code wins)
ABAC onboarding resource + division scoping IMPLEMENTED abac.py:119, 148, 164, 174–175
Web board / record detail / self‑view IMPLEMENTED web.py:395–483; onboarding.html, onboarding_detail.html; nav base.html:37–40
In‑page actions (sign / mark / release on the page) PLANNED Templates are read‑only display; all mutations are via the onboarding service. HTMX is loaded but unused for these.
Accessibility (semantic tables, captions, scope) IMPLEMENTED onboarding*.html use <th scope>, <caption class="sr-only">, aria-labelledby
P4 NEXUS chat tools (T28/T29) PLANNED Deferred per PDS §10; no tools registered

Mode used: auto. The guide body documents IMPLEMENTED capabilities in present tense and marks PARTIAL/PLANNED items with a banner.

Appendix B — Policy traceability

Workflow → policy section, from the PDS §5.3 traceability table.

Appendix B — Policy traceability — data table 11
Policy § Onboarding capability
§3 definitions, §5 phases Record lifecycle and phase timeline (4.1, 4.2, 3)
§5.1 Pre‑boarding Preboarding tasks — packet, screening init, provisioning prep, day‑one plan (4.3)
§5.2 Day 1 / §5.3 Week 1 Day‑one and week‑one tasks; acknowledgments (4.3, 4.4)
§5.4 30/60/90 Milestone check‑ins; reminders (4.9, 4.10)
§6 I‑9 Statutory I‑9 §1 and §2 tasks — non‑waivable, priority reminders (4.3)
§6 E‑Verify E‑Verify case task/screening — off until configured (4.1)
§7 acknowledgments Versioned acknowledgment ledger (4.4)
§8 screening / HSPD‑12 Screening processes; dependent‑task blocking (4.7)
§9 GovCon training Ethics, mandatory disclosure, anti‑kickback courses (4.5)
§9 DCAA timekeeping DCAA course + time‑charging gate; blocks timecards (4.5, 4.6)
§10 SCA / DBA Labor‑standards orientation + wage‑determination task (4.1, 4.5)
§11 AUP / MFA IT Acceptable‑Use acknowledgment; provisioning/MFA tasks (4.4, 4.3)
§11 CUI / CMMC training gate CUI Handling + CMMC‑NIST courses; CUI‑provisioning gate (4.5, 4.6)
§12 EEO / VEVRAA / 503 EEO & Anti‑Harassment Orientation (4.5)
§13 safety Safety Orientation / OSHA; PPE and site tasks; field flag (4.3, 4.5)
§14 provisioning / assets Least‑privilege provisioning tasks; asset issuance + recovery (4.3, 4.8)
§15 benefits Benefits‑enrollment task (4.3)
§16 records / retention Pointers‑only design; retention kept as configuration (1, 4.13)
§17 compliance / audit Chain‑anchored sign‑offs; completion certificate; exports (4.11, 4.12)
§20 attachments A/B/C Checklist / acknowledgment / training views and CSV exports (4.2, 4.12)

Policy references are carried as citations only; this guide is product documentation, not legal advice. Several program values (passing scores, retention periods, document titles/versions, clause citations, E‑Verify applicability) await HR/legal confirmation and are maintained in the program configuration.

Appendix C — Change log

Appendix C — Change log — data table 12
Version Date Notes
1.0 2026‑06‑10 First release. Documents the Onboarding module as built through P1–P3 (records, tasks, acknowledgments, training, gates, screening, assets, check‑ins, completion, cancellation, reminders, exports, web board/detail/self‑view) plus the auto‑create‑on‑hire and separation hooks. PLANNED items: manual per‑task waiver, in‑page self‑service actions, CUI‑gate downstream enforcement, the sweep’s automatic schedule, and NEXUS chat tools.

TTI‑Solutions — Infrastructure with Integrity.

TTI-Solutions — Infrastructure with Integrity · Guide v1.0 · 2026-06-10
